_doc: Generic content for the eight-meeting operating rhythm. Shared between the DBOS company-state page (dbos.darlison.com/meetings) and the public-facing reference block on https://www.darlison.com/meeting-cadence-assessment/. Single source of truth — edit here, both surfaces update on next page load. Mirrored automatically to ~/darlison-downloads/meeting-cadence-content.json (public via raw.githubusercontent.com) by the ai-config auto-sync hook.

principles.name: Cadence is a ritual, not a calendar.
principles.oneLine: A meeting is a ritual the team can predict, not a recurring slot they tolerate.
principles.why: When a meeting is treated as a calendar slot, it decays. Ritual implies preparation, repetition, and respect for the form. The rhythm creates the discipline, and the discipline creates the momentum.
inPractice: Same time, same agenda each cycle.
If you're not early, you're late.
Skipping a meeting once is a decision; skipping twice is a pattern.
If you cannot answer "what would break if we cancelled this for a quarter," retire it.

principles.name: Do not mix status meetings with decide meetings.
principles.oneLine: Routing and decision-making do not share a room.
principles.why: Status meetings are fast and predictable. Decide meetings need preparation and time. Mixing them slows the status read and starves the decisions.
inPractice: Daily Huddle and Weekly Leadership Team Meeting are status. They route, they do not solve.
Quarterly and Annual are decide meetings. That is where the next plan gets built and the calls get made.
Off-track items leave the Weekly Leadership Meeting routed (single-function correction, cross-function structural change, or a named working group with a decide-by date), not solved live.

principles.name: Prepare before you sit down.
principles.oneLine: The meeting starts where preparation ends.
principles.why: If preparation happens in the meeting, the first 30 minutes is consumed by status updates instead of decisions. The discipline is in the prep, not the meeting.
inPractice: Numbers are current before everyone sits down.
Decide meetings assume the prep package has been read.
Anyone bringing a problem brings the analysis with them, not just the question.
How the prep happens is up to the company. The discipline is the prep, not the format.

principles.name: Good news opens every meeting.
principles.why: The Cohesive and Cultural systems get reinforced every time the team sits down together. Good News is non-negotiable in all eight meetings.
inPractice: Every one of the eight meetings opens with good news.
Six of eight also include values recognition or discussion.
These two rows are how culture lives in the rhythm, not in posters.

principles.name: Share hard problems before the meeting, not in it.
principles.oneLine: Write it down first. Meet only when writing won't get you there.
principles.why: Complex topics hit people on the fly with no time to think. Loudest voice wins, others stare at the ceiling, no decisions get made. When you share the problem in writing first, whether a doc, a memo, or a short video, people can read it on their own time, opt out if they have nothing to add, and walk in with their thinking already done.
inPractice: Share the problem in writing first. A doc, a memo, a short video. Anything that lets people read it before the meeting.
Use the meeting itself for hashing out conflict, clearing up confusion, or making the final call.
A small working group with a decide-by date often beats convening the full leadership team.

principles.name: Master the foundation before extending.
principles.oneLine: Add meetings sequentially as the prior habit holds.
principles.why: A team that has not internalized the Daily Huddle will not sustain the Quarterly. Layered cadences fail when the base layer is shaky. Master this first, then extend where the business needs it.
inPractice: Implementation order: Daily Huddle, Weekly Leadership, Weekly One-on-One, Monthly + Town Hall, Quarterly + Quarterly Scorecard Review, Annual.
The full Quarterly agenda is the destination, not the starting point.
Once the eight are running, layer in the rituals your business needs (retros, hackathons, product councils).

meetings.name: Daily Huddle
meetings.oneLine: Status. A few minutes a day to connect the team to the plan.
meetings.purpose: Keep the team connected to the plan every day. The most important meeting in the system. Everything else builds on it.
agenda: Good news and values recognition (30 seconds)
Are you on track? (yes / no)
Stuck and need help? (yes / no)
Top priority for today

meetings.name: Weekly Leadership Team Meeting
meetings.oneLine: Status. A weekly review of the critical numbers and progress on the quarter's goals.
meetings.purpose: Review the critical numbers and progress on the quarter's goals. Surface what is off-track and route the fix. Status, not problem-solving.
prep: Critical numbers are current before the meeting starts.
Anyone owning an off-track number posts the proposed situation, cause, correction and followup plan at least 1 business day before the meeting.
Every attendee has reviewed the scoreboard, the written reports, and any followups due this week.
agenda: Good news
Values recognition
Scoreboard review
Quarterly Highly Achievable Goal [QHAG] status
Priority and Sprint Lane status
Dynamic: stucks, asks, offers
What Do You Recommend [WDYR]. The leadership coaching practice.

meetings.name: Weekly CEO Email
meetings.oneLine: Cascade. A weekly email update from the CEO to the whole company.
meetings.purpose: Keep the whole company connected to the plan and the leadership team's thinking between meetings.
meetings.note: Not a meeting. A written ritual that pairs with the Weekly Leadership Team Meeting.

meetings.name: Weekly One-on-One
meetings.oneLine: Coaching. The person's meeting, not yours. Coach the individual.
meetings.purpose: Coach the individual. Not status. Not task management. Coaching.
agenda: Good news
Values discussion
What is on your mind? (the person being coached sets direction)
Question funnel: explore, then converge on action
Improvement check-in from the last Quarterly Scorecard Review
What Do You Recommend [WDYR]. The leadership coaching practice.
Commitments and next steps

meetings.name: Monthly Meeting
meetings.oneLine: Status. How are we tracking against the 36-month rolling forecast, and has anything strategic changed that is affecting performance?
meetings.purpose: Review the 36-month rolling forecast against actual performance. Compare what the team said it would deliver to what it has actually delivered. Then identify anything strategic that has changed and is now affecting the trajectory.
agenda: Good news
Performance against the 36-month rolling forecast. What did we say we would do, and what have we actually done?
Strategic check-in. What has changed in the market, the Swimlanes, or the assumptions behind the forecast that is affecting performance? Surface what needs attention at the next quarterly.

meetings.name: Monthly Town Hall
meetings.oneLine: Cascade. Connect the whole company to the plan, the numbers, and the culture.
meetings.purpose: Keep the whole company connected to the plan, the numbers, and the culture.
agenda: Good news
Company-wide values recognition
Theme progress: quarterly goal status and what everyone can contribute
Financial transparency: what is working, what is not
One improvement action for financial health

meetings.name: Quarterly Meeting
meetings.oneLine: Decide. Touch every system. Build the next 90-day plan.
meetings.purpose: Review all six systems. Build the next 90-day plan.
prep: Every leader's Sprint Lanes from the past quarter are up to date.
Every leader is ready to recommend what the next quarter needs to advance the company toward its 3 Year Highly Achievable Goal [3HAG].
agenda: Cohesive: good news, cohesive question
Cultural: Core Purpose, Core Values, Big Hairy Audacious Goal [BHAG], Profit/X
Human: Key Function Flow Map [KFFM], Functional Organization Chart, A-Player team assessment, What Do You Recommend [WDYR] check-in
Strategy: 3 Year Highly Achievable Goal [3HAG] review and validate, Market Map, Core Customer, Attribution Framework, Swimlanes, Brand Promise
Execution: Scoreboard, 1 Year Highly Achievable Goal [1HAG] status, Quarterly Highly Achievable Goal [QHAG] status, Priority and Sprint Lane status, new QHAG and 13-week Sprint Lanes
Cash: widget forecast vs actual, 36-month rolling forecast
Close: meeting effectiveness review, Three Cascading Messages, One-Phrase Close
meetings.note: The full agenda is the destination, not the starting point. Add elements quarter by quarter as the team grows into them.

meetings.name: Annual Meeting
meetings.oneLine: Decide. Validate strategy, set the new three-year goal, and build the annual plan.
meetings.purpose: Validate strategy. Set the new 3 Year Highly Achievable Goal [3HAG]. Build the annual plan.
agenda: Day 1 (Strategy). Same flow as Quarterly through Cohesive, Cultural, Human, and Strategy systems, with greater depth. Five Dysfunctions assessment. Key Function Flow Map [KFFM] reviewed at Levels 1 and 2. Market Map and Core Customer refreshed. Attribution Map, Activity Fit Map (Differentiators), Swimlanes, and the 3 Year Highly Achievable Goal [3HAG] are rebuilt from scratch to force fresh thinking.

Day 2 (Execution). Current 1 Year Highly Achievable Goal [1HAG] and Quarterly Highly Achievable Goal [QHAG] status. New 1HAG and QHAG with 13-week Sprint Lanes, rebuilt to align with the new 3HAG. Full Cash review with the 36-month rolling forecast extended one more year. Metronomics Survey review. Three Cascading Messages. One-Phrase Close.

meetings.name: Quarterly Scorecard Review
meetings.oneLine: Coaching. Every 90 days, a leader and a direct report review the scorecard together and agree on a Continuous Improvement Plan for the next quarter.
meetings.purpose: A two-way conversation, held every 90 days, between a leader and a direct report. They review the scorecard together (mission, critical number, supporting metrics, competencies, and values) and build the Continuous Improvement Plan for the next quarter. This replaces the annual review entirely.
agenda: Scorecard review. Cover mission, critical number, supporting metrics, competencies, and values together.
Skip-level review findings, where applicable. Input from the manager's manager about the team's experience of the manager.
Continuous Improvement Plan [CIP]. The most important output of the meeting. Agree on one improvement focus for the next 90 days.
meetings.note: The CIP is checked weekly in the One-on-One and revisited at the next Quarterly Scorecard Review.
